mentions.role_in_text: Harvard-led team demonstrates a 256-qubit programmable quantum simulator
mentions.occurrence_sentence: Founder Mikhail Lukin's Harvard-led team demonstrates a 256-qubit programmable quantum simulator—an inflection point for neutral-atom computing that underpins QuEra's platform.
QuEra and Harvard researchers publish groundbreaking research in Nature describing a method for cutting logical-layer runtime overheads by ~10–100×.
Grounded in pioneering research from QuEra and our academic collaborators Harvard and MIT, our roadmap charts a definitive path to quantum value.

In collaboration with Harvard and MIT, QuEra demonstrated logical-level magic-state distillation entirely within the logical qubit layer on Gemini, building error-protected logical qubits using distance-3 and distance-5 color codes and a 5-to-1 distillation protocol.

In collaboration with Harvard and MIT, QuEra demonstrated logical-level magic-state distillation entirely within the logical qubit layer on Gemini, building error-protected logical qubits using distance-3 and distance-5 color codes and a 5-to-1 distillation protocol.

mentions.role_in_text: Collaborator in quantum error correction research.
mentions.occurrence_sentence: QuEra, Harvard, MIT and additional collaborators have achieved several QEC milestones that underscore our leadership in the field.

mentions.role_in_text: Collaborator in quantum error correction research
mentions.occurrence_sentence: Neutral-atom platforms

In this work led by our QEC team, in collaboration with Harvard and Yale researchers, we propose a novel framework for transversal algorithmic fault tolerance in quantum computing, focusing on reducing the overall space-time volume required for computations.

In our collaboration with researchers at Harvard, Chicago, Caltech, and Arizona, we propose physical realizations of high-rate quantum low-density parity-check (qLDPC) codes, which hold promise in reducing the space overhead of QEC, using currently available tools in neutral atom arrays.

In our collaboration with researchers at Harvard, Chicago, Caltech, and Arizona, we propose physical realizations of high-rate quantum low-density parity-check (qLDPC) codes, which hold promise in reducing the space overhead of QEC, using currently available tools in neutral atom arrays.
In this collaboration led by Harvard University, we explore the use of correlated decoding strategies for logical quantum algorithms implemented with transversal gates.

This paper, led by our collaborators at Harvard and experimentally carried out in the first atom array experiment system at Harvard, presents the groundbreaking demonstration of a logical quantum processor using reconfigurable atom arrays.

This paper, led by our collaborators at Harvard and experimentally carried out in the first atom array experiment system at Harvard, presents the groundbreaking demonstration of a logical quantum processor using reconfigurable atom arrays.
